description: Taxonomy of asset types found in cloud environments, with security characteristics per type. Used to drive asset inventory, risk assessment, and control applicability. Each asset type maps to one or more CSA Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) v4 domains.

examples.gcp: Compute Engine (e2, n2, c2 machine types)

securityProfile.attackSurface: Full operating system attack surface — kernel vulnerabilities, unpatched services, SSH/RDP exposure, instance metadata service (IMDS), user data scripts containing secrets
keyRisks: Unpatched OS and middleware creating exploitable CVEs
Instance metadata SSRF enabling IAM credential theft (IMDSv1)
Overprivileged instance profiles granting broad AWS/Azure/GCP access
SSH key sprawl with no rotation or centralised management
AMI/image templates containing embedded credentials or secrets
compensatingControls: Enforce IMDSv2 (AWS) / disable metadata for unnecessary instances
CIS Benchmark hardened golden images with automated patching
Host-based EDR (CrowdStrike, Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne)
No SSH/RDP — use Systems Manager Session Manager or Azure Bastion
Least-privilege instance roles scoped to specific resources
VPC Flow Logs and OS audit logs forwarded to SIEM

examples.aws: ECS (Fargate / EC2 launch type), EKS (managed Kubernetes)
examples.azure: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service), Azure Container Instances

securityProfile.attackSurface: Container image vulnerabilities, Kubernetes API server exposure, pod-to-pod lateral movement, container escape via kernel exploits, supply chain through base images and registries
keyRisks: Vulnerable base images with known CVEs pulled from public registries
Kubernetes API server exposed to internet without authentication
Privileged containers running with host PID/network namespace access
Secrets mounted as environment variables visible in container inspect
Container escape exploits (runc, containerd CVEs) reaching host OS
Kubernetes RBAC misconfiguration granting cluster-admin to service accounts
compensatingControls: Image scanning in CI/CD pipeline and at registry admission (ECR scanning, Trivy, Snyk)
Pod Security Standards enforcement (restricted profile)
Network policies for pod-to-pod micro-segmentation (Calico, Cilium)
Kubernetes Secrets encrypted with KMS (etcd encryption at rest)
Binary Authorization / image signing for deployment gate
Runtime security monitoring (Falco, GuardDuty EKS Runtime Monitoring)
Private cluster with no public API endpoint

securityProfile.attackSurface: Function code vulnerabilities, event injection through triggers, overprivileged execution roles, dependency supply chain attacks, cold start credential caching
keyRisks: Overprivileged function execution roles (Lambda role with s3:* or admin access)
Event injection — untrusted data from S3, SQS, API Gateway triggers exploiting function logic
Dependency confusion or supply chain attacks in function deployment packages
Secrets stored in environment variables without Secrets Manager integration
Insufficient logging — security events not captured in ephemeral execution
compensatingControls: One IAM role per function with minimum required permissions
Input validation on all event sources — treat every trigger as untrusted input
Secrets Manager integration for all credentials (never plaintext env vars)
Function code scanning (SAST, SCA) in CI/CD pipeline
VPC attachment for functions accessing private resources
Distributed tracing (X-Ray, Application Insights) for security observability

securityProfile.attackSurface: Public access misconfiguration (the most common cloud security finding), overly permissive bucket policies, pre-signed URL abuse, cross-account access, data exfiltration through legitimate API calls
keyRisks: Publicly accessible buckets exposing sensitive data (personal data, credentials, backups)
Bucket policies allowing s3:GetObject from Principal: * (any AWS account or anonymous)
Pre-signed URLs with excessive expiry enabling persistent unauthorised access
Object versioning disabled — no recovery from ransomware or accidental deletion
Cross-account access without proper IAM boundary enforcement
Logging disabled — no visibility into who accessed which objects
compensatingControls: S3 Block Public Access enabled at account/organisation level (AWS SCP enforcement)
Encryption at rest with customer-managed KMS keys (SSE-KMS)
Bucket policies with explicit deny for unencrypted uploads
Object versioning and MFA Delete for critical buckets
S3 Access Logs and CloudTrail S3 data events enabled
Macie / Purview / SCC for sensitive data classification
VPC endpoint (gateway) for S3 access — no internet path

securityProfile.attackSurface: Unencrypted volumes containing sensitive data, snapshot sharing across accounts, abandoned volumes with residual data, snapshot copying to attacker-controlled accounts
keyRisks: Unencrypted EBS volumes containing database files, credentials, or PII
EBS snapshots shared publicly or with unintended AWS accounts
Detached (orphaned) volumes retaining sensitive data after instance termination
Root volume snapshots containing SSH keys, cloud-init secrets, and application credentials
compensatingControls: Default EBS encryption at account level (AWS SCP requiring encrypted volumes)
Snapshot sharing restricted to approved accounts only
Automated cleanup of detached volumes (Lambda-based or Config rule)
KMS key policies restricting who can use encryption keys for snapshot access
Regular audit of snapshot permissions across organisation

examples.aws: RDS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle), Aurora, DynamoDB, ElastiCache
examples.azure: Azure SQL Database, Azure Cosmos DB, Azure Database for PostgreSQL/MySQL, Azure Cache for Redis
examples.gcp: Cloud SQL, Cloud Spanner, Firestore, Bigtable, Memorystore

securityProfile.attackSurface: Public endpoint exposure, SQL injection through application layer, weak database authentication, unencrypted connections, overprivileged database users, snapshot exposure
keyRisks: Database instance with public accessibility enabled (reachable from internet)
SQL injection vulnerabilities in application code reaching managed database
Master/admin credentials stored in application code or configuration files
Database connections without TLS encryption (data in transit exposure)
Automated backups/snapshots not encrypted or shared too broadly
Database audit logging disabled — no visibility into data access patterns
compensatingControls: Private endpoint/subnet only — no public accessibility
Database credentials in Secrets Manager with automatic rotation
TLS enforcement for all database connections (rds.force_ssl, require_secure_transport)
Database audit logging enabled and forwarded to SIEM (RDS Enhanced Monitoring, Azure SQL Auditing)
Application-level parameterised queries to prevent SQL injection
Database user least-privilege — no application accounts with DBA privileges
Backup encryption with customer-managed KMS keys

assetTypes.name: IAM Roles, Policies, and Service Accounts

examples.aws: IAM Roles, IAM Policies (inline/managed), IAM Users, STS Assumed Roles
examples.azure: Entra ID Roles, Azure RBAC Role Assignments, Managed Identities, Service Principals
examples.gcp: IAM Roles (predefined/custom), Service Accounts, Workload Identity Federation
examples.alibaba: RAM Roles, RAM Policies, RAM Users

securityProfile.attackSurface: Overprivileged roles and policies enabling escalation, long-lived credentials, cross-account trust misconfiguration, service account key exposure, IAM policy complexity creating unintended permissions
keyRisks: IAM policies with Action: * and Resource: * (admin-equivalent permissions)
Long-lived IAM access keys stored in code, CI/CD, or shared across teams
Cross-account role trust policies with overly broad Principal conditions
Unused IAM users and roles accumulating unnecessary permissions over time
IAM policy conditions bypassed through resource-based policies
Service account keys downloaded and stored insecurely
compensatingControls: IAM Access Analyzer / Entra Permissions Management for permission auditing
No long-lived access keys — use IAM roles, managed identities, workload identity federation
Service Control Policies (SCPs) as organisation-level guardrails
Regular access reviews with automated revocation of unused permissions
Custom IAM policies with explicit deny for sensitive operations
Credential rotation enforcement (90 days maximum for any long-lived credential)

examples.aws: VPC, Subnets (public/private), Route Tables, Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway
examples.azure: VNet, Subnets, Route Tables, Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway
examples.gcp: VPC Network (auto-mode/custom-mode), Subnets, Cloud Router, Cloud NAT
examples.alibaba: VPC, VSwitch, Route Tables, NAT Gateway

securityProfile.attackSurface: Overly permissive routing enabling internet access from private resources, flat network architecture without segmentation, VPC peering creating uncontrolled lateral movement paths, DNS-based data exfiltration
keyRisks: Default VPC used for production workloads (all subnets public by default in AWS)
Resources placed in public subnets unnecessarily (databases, internal APIs)
VPC peering with full CIDR routing enabling cross-environment lateral movement
Missing VPC Flow Logs — no network traffic visibility
Route table misconfiguration sending private traffic through internet gateway
compensatingControls: Delete default VPC — use purpose-built VPCs with explicit subnet design
Private subnets for all non-edge workloads, public subnets only for load balancers
VPC Flow Logs enabled (all traffic, not just rejected) and forwarded to SIEM
DNS query logging enabled for exfiltration detection
Transit Gateway with route domain segmentation for multi-VPC environments
Environment isolation (separate VPCs for production, staging, development)

examples.aws: Application Load Balancer (ALB), Network Load Balancer (NLB), Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
examples.azure: Azure Application Gateway, Azure Load Balancer, Azure Front Door
examples.gcp: Google Cloud Load Balancing (HTTP(S), TCP/UDP, Internal)
examples.alibaba: Server Load Balancer (SLB), Application Load Balancer (ALB)

securityProfile.attackSurface: TLS termination configuration, health check endpoint exposure, access log gaps, backend target group misconfiguration
keyRisks: Outdated TLS configuration (TLS 1.0/1.1, weak cipher suites)
Health check endpoints exposing internal application state or version information
Access logs disabled — no visibility into request patterns or attack attempts
Backend targets directly reachable from internet, bypassing load balancer security
compensatingControls: TLS 1.2+ only with modern cipher suites (ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13 or equivalent)
Access logging enabled for all load balancers (ALB access logs to S3)
Security group on backend targets allowing only load balancer source
WAF integration on application load balancers
SSL certificate automation via ACM / Azure Key Vault / Google-managed certificates

examples.aws: Amazon API Gateway (REST, HTTP, WebSocket), AppSync (GraphQL)
examples.azure: Azure API Management, Azure Front Door (with routing rules)

securityProfile.attackSurface: Unauthenticated API endpoints, missing rate limiting enabling abuse, overly permissive CORS, request/response logging gaps, backend API direct access bypassing gateway
keyRisks: API endpoints deployed without authentication (public APIs returning sensitive data)
No rate limiting enabling credential stuffing, scraping, or DDoS at API level
CORS wildcard (*) allowing any origin to call the API
GraphQL introspection enabled in production revealing full schema to attackers
API keys embedded in client-side code (mobile apps, JavaScript)
compensatingControls: OAuth 2.0 / JWT authentication on all API endpoints
Rate limiting and throttling per client / per API key
Request validation (OpenAPI schema validation at gateway)
CORS restricted to specific permitted origins
API key rotation and usage monitoring
GraphQL introspection disabled in production
WAF rules on API gateway for injection and abuse prevention

examples.azure: Azure DNS, Azure Private DNS Zones, Azure Traffic Manager
examples.gcp: Google Cloud DNS, Google Cloud Domains

securityProfile.attackSurface: DNS hijacking, subdomain takeover via dangling CNAME records, DNS tunnelling for data exfiltration, zone transfer exposure
keyRisks: Subdomain takeover — CNAME pointing to deprovisioned cloud resource (S3, Azure Blob, Heroku)
DNSSEC not enabled, allowing DNS spoofing and cache poisoning
DNS query logging disabled — no visibility into DNS-based exfiltration or C2 communication
DNS zone accessible to overly broad IAM roles enabling record modification
compensatingControls: Regular subdomain audit to detect dangling CNAME/A records
DNSSEC enabled for all public hosted zones
Route 53 Resolver Query Logging / DNS query logging forwarded to SIEM
IAM policies restricting DNS zone modification to specific roles
DNS firewall (Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall) blocking known malicious domains

securityProfile.attackSurface: CDN caching sensitive content, origin IP exposure, TLS misconfiguration, cache poisoning
keyRisks: Sensitive API responses cached at CDN edge (authentication tokens, PII)
Origin server IP leaked through DNS history or error pages, enabling CDN bypass
TLS certificate on CDN not matching backend, creating downgrade opportunities
Cache poisoning through manipulated Host headers or query parameters
compensatingControls: Cache-Control headers preventing caching of sensitive responses
Origin Access Control (OAC) ensuring only CDN can reach origin (AWS S3 OAC, CloudFront signed URLs)
Custom origin headers validated by backend (shared secret between CDN and origin)
HTTPS-only with HSTS headers
CDN access logs enabled and monitored for anomalous patterns

examples.aws: SQS (Simple Queue Service), SNS (Simple Notification Service), EventBridge, Kinesis
examples.azure: Azure Service Bus, Azure Event Grid, Azure Event Hubs

securityProfile.attackSurface: Message injection, queue policy misconfiguration allowing cross-account access, unencrypted messages in transit, poison message DoS
keyRisks: Queue/topic policies allowing SendMessage from any AWS account (Principal: *)
Messages containing sensitive data transmitted without encryption
Dead-letter queues accumulating sensitive messages without access controls
Event injection — attacker publishing malicious events that trigger downstream processing
compensatingControls: Queue/topic policies with explicit Principal restrictions
Server-side encryption for all queues and topics (SSE-SQS, SSE-KMS)
VPC endpoints for queue access — no internet path
Dead-letter queue monitoring and alerting
Input validation on all message consumers — treat messages as untrusted

examples.aws: AWS KMS (Key Management Service), AWS CloudHSM
examples.azure: Azure Key Vault, Azure Managed HSM, Azure Dedicated HSM
examples.gcp: Google Cloud KMS, Cloud HSM, Cloud External Key Manager (EKM)
examples.alibaba: Alibaba Cloud KMS, Data Encryption Service

securityProfile.attackSurface: Key policy misconfiguration granting overly broad access, key deletion removing decryption capability, cross-account key sharing without controls
keyRisks: KMS key policy allowing kms:* to broad principals (any role in account can use/manage keys)
Scheduled key deletion without impact assessment (data becomes permanently undecryptable)
Customer-managed keys (CMK) not used — relying on AWS/Azure/GCP-managed keys without customer control
Key rotation not enabled — same key material used indefinitely
KMS key grants accumulating and not cleaned up
compensatingControls: Least-privilege key policies — separate key users from key administrators
Automatic key rotation enabled (annual minimum)
Key deletion protection with multi-day waiting period (7-30 days)
CloudTrail logging of all KMS API calls for audit
Separate KMS keys per workload/environment (production keys isolated from development)
Customer-managed keys for all regulated data

examples.gcp: Google Artifact Registry, Google Container Registry (deprecated)

securityProfile.attackSurface: Malicious image injection, vulnerable base images, image tag mutability enabling supply chain attacks, cross-account image pulling without authentication
keyRisks: Mutable image tags — attacker replaces 'latest' tag with backdoored image
No image scanning — vulnerabilities in base images deployed to production
Public registry access allowing anyone to pull (and potentially push) images
Images containing embedded secrets (API keys, database credentials) in layers
Unsigned images deployed without provenance verification
compensatingControls: Image immutability (immutable tags) preventing tag overwrite
Automated vulnerability scanning on push (ECR scanning, ACR Defender, Artifact Analysis)
Image signing and verification (cosign, Notary, AWS Signer)
Private registry with authentication required for pull and push
Registry replication for cross-region availability with consistent security policy
Lifecycle policies to clean up untagged and old images
